title: Four-shaft huck lace
subtitle: Weaving the traditional Swedish lace structure in cotton or linen on a four-shaft loom
excerpt: Huck lace is a traditional Scandinavian lace weave that produces a fabric with both texture and visual openness by alternating tabby and float sheds. This entry covers the threading, tie-up, and treadling for a standard four-shaft huck sample, with guidance on sett and yarn choice.
sourceNotes: Huck lace is documented in Stickney, Handweaving (1924, public domain, Internet Archive), and described in Harriet Tidball, Handweaving Patterns from Finland (1966, in-copyright, substance only referenced). The structure originates in Scandinavian table-linen weaving traditions of the 17th and 18th centuries.

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern by which each warp thread is assigned to a specific shaft. In huck lace, the threading uses both ground and pattern shafts in a fixed alternation.
A thread that passes over two or more opposing threads without interlacing. In huck lace, warp floats and weft floats create the open lace holes at the block intersections.
The thread passed across the width of the loom through each shed. In huck lace, each weft pick either weaves tabby or creates a float that opens a lace hole.

content.text: Huck lace is a traditional Scandinavian structure used in table linen and towelling for centuries. It alternates tabby picks with pattern picks that create controlled
level and the warp level. The cloth has a pebbly surface in ground areas and open holes at the block corners. A fine linen or cotton at an open
sett shows the lace most clearly.

items.name: Fine linen or cotton warp yarn (16/2 or 20/2)
Same or slightly finer weft yarn
Four-shaft floor loom with at least four treadles

content.text: Standard four-shaft huck threading unit: 1-2-1-3 repeated across the warp. Shaft 1 is the ground shaft appearing in every unit. Shafts 2 and 3 carry the pattern threads for the two blocks; shaft 4 is used for the tie-down picks. Sett at approximately 60 to 65% of the balanced plain-weave sett for the same yarn to allow the lace holes to open.

Tie: treadle 1 to shafts 1 and 3 (tabby A), treadle 2 to shafts 2 and 4 (tabby B), treadle 3 to shaft 2 (pattern A float), treadle 4 to shaft 3 (pattern B float). Treadle sequence for one pattern repeat: 1, 3, 2, 3, 1, 3, 2, 3 (block A open), then 1, 4, 2, 4, 1, 4, 2, 4 (block B open). Alternate tabby treadles between each pattern treadle.

Huck lace in linen will become more defined after wet-finishing. Wash in warm water, pull gently to size while damp, and press firmly from the reverse side with a hot iron while still slightly damp. The lace holes will open more clearly after pressing than in the loom state.

items.problem: The lace holes are not visible in the finished sample
items.cause: The sett is too close or the yarn is too thick for the float length
items.fix: Resample at a 20 to 30% more open sett. Huck lace is very sensitive to sett; the standard advice is to start with a sett 35% more open than the balanced weave sett for the yarn.

items.problem: The tabby picks do not beat to the same level as the pattern picks
items.cause: Pattern picks and tabby picks require different amounts of weft per inch due to the float structure
items.fix: Beat pattern picks slightly less firmly than tabby picks to compensate. The floats need less weft to fill the same width because they travel over rather than under warp threads.
